'''Clint Newton''' (born 18 June 1981) is an American born and former international [[rugby league]] footballer who played as a {{rlp|sr}} and {{rlp|lk}}. He played for the [[Newcastle Knights]], [[Melbourne Storm]] and [[Penrith Panthers]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]], [[Hull Kingston Rovers]] in the [[Super League]], [[Country New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales Country]] and the [[United States national rugby league team|United States]] at representative level as well as also having a stint in rugby union for Avoca Beach Rugby Club on the Central Coast, NSW.

Newton represented the [[United States national rugby league team|United States]] in their [[2013 Rugby League World Cup]] campaign.

==Early years==
Clint Newton is the son of Jackie and [[professional golfer]] [[Jack Newton]] and the brother of professional golfer Kristie Newton. He was born in [[Myrtle Beach, South Carolina]].<ref name="Scotland bid fails for Newton">{{cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/08/05/16/RUGBYL_Newton.html|title=Scotland bid fails for Newton|publisher=Sportinglife|date=16 May 2008|access-date=16 May 2008}}</ref>

The Newtons moved back to Jack's native [[Newcastle, New South Wales]] where Clint began playing rugby league with the Valentine-Eleebana junior rugby league club.

During his junior years Clint was coached by former Newcastle Knights captain Sam Stewart.

==Newcastle Knights==
Newton made his first grade début coming off the bench in [[Newcastle Knights]] 45–24 victory over [[New Zealand Warriors]] in Round 7 of the 2001 NRL season.  Newton was not involved in Newcastles' [[NRL season 2001#Grand final|Grand Final]] win that season, with his seventh and final appearance of the season coming in the Round 23 loss to [[Wests Tigers]] where he suffered a season ending shoulder injury.

After recovering from his shoulder injury he gained selection in the Knights first team for the [[2002 World Club Challenge]] against the Bradford Bulls.  Newton was then selected for Newcastles' 38–12 Round 1 victory against Northern Eagles, scoring his first try in the game.  Newton went on to appear regularly for Newcastle during the 2002 season, making 25 appearances, mainly from the bench, and scoring 4 tries.  However it wasn't until Round 7 of the 2004 season that Newton held down a regular first team starting role after suffering two severe ankle injuries in 2003.

In 2006 he was selected to play Country Origin, but was ruled out with injury and replaced by the man he struck in the elbow late in the 2004 season, [[Ashton Sims]] (see below). Sims himself was also ruled out and replaced by then-Wests Tigers backrower [[Anthony Laffranchi]].

===Ashton Sims incident===
During the 2004 season, Newton was sent off in the Round 24 match against St George Illawarra Dragons.  Newton received a 12-week [[suspension (punishment)|suspension]] for elbowing [[St George Illawarra Dragons]] forward [[Ashton Sims]].<ref>{{cite news|first=Brad|last=Walter|title=Newton struck out for 12 weeks|url=http://www.smh.com.au/articles/2004/08/25/1093246621615.html|work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|date=26 August 2004|access-date=31 May 2007}}</ref> The suspension cost Newton the chance to represent the [[United States national rugby league team|United States]] in an international match against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] later that year.

Newton had been sin binned four weeks earlier against the [[North Queensland Cowboys|Cowboys]] for a professional foul.

===Departure===
[[File:Clint Newton and Ryan Hoffman in 2007.JPG|250px|thumb|right|[[Ryan Hoffman]] and Newton in 2007 celebrating after a win for Melbourne]]
On 27 May 2007, Newton started in Newcastle Knights 71–6 defeat by [[Brisbane Broncos]] in what turned out to be his last game for the club. The next day, Newton announced he was leaving the Newcastle Knights after just 100 games, due to "personal reasons" and after learning coach, Brian Smith no longer saw him as a "must keep" for the remainder of the 2007 season or beyond.<ref>{{cite news|title=Newton shocks Knights in day of changes|url=http://www.smh.com.au/news/Sport/Newton-shocks-Knights-in-day-of-changes/2007/05/28/1180205154921.html|work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|date=28 May 2007|access-date=31 May 2007}}</ref>

==Melbourne Storm==
On 29 May 2007, Newton signed with the [[Melbourne Storm]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Storm signs Newton after Knights release|url=http://news.leaguehq.com.au/storm-signs-newton-after-knights-release/20073929-fhi.html|work=[[The Sydney Morning Herald]]|date=29 May 2007|access-date=31 May 2007|archive-date=27 September 2007|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070927012104/http://news.leaguehq.com.au/storm-signs-newton-after-knights-release/20073929-fhi.html|url-status=dead}}</ref> He started in the second row in the Storm's round 13 clash with the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in Auckland and was one of the best players on the field, playing the full 80 minutes.

He went on to play and score in the [[NRL season 2007#Grand final|Grand Final]], a 34–8 victory over Manly, becoming the second player to transfer mid-year to a Grand Final winning club and made history by being the first player to transfer mid-year and score a try in his winning grand final. In all Newton played 15 matches for Melbourne, ending up on the losing side only once, a Round 20 defeat by Sydney Roosters. He had the same birthdays as fellow Storm players [[Billy Slater]] and [[Cameron Smith (rugby league, born 1983)|Cameron Smith]] but was two years older than both players. In 2007, the premiership that Newton won with Melbourne was stripped by the NRL for [[Melbourne Storm salary cap breach|major breaches of the salary cap]] alongside the 2009 premiership.<ref>{{Cite news|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/2010-04-23/storm-punishment-too-harsh-geyer/407390|title = Storm punishment too harsh: Geyer|publisher = ABC News|location=Australia|date = 22 April 2010|last1 = Images|first1 = Getty}}</ref>

==Hull KR==
On 17 October 2007, he was released by the [[Melbourne Storm]] due to salary cap restrictions and announced his move to the [[Super League]] on a two-year contract at [[Hull Kingston Rovers]].<ref>{{cite news|title=Newton joins the Super League|url=http://www.foxsports.com.au/story/0,8659,22600341-23214,00.html|publisher=[[Fox Sports News (Australia)|Fox Sports News]]|date=17 October 2007|access-date=17 October 2007}}</ref>

===2008===
Newton made his Hull KR début in the opening match of the 2008 season, a narrow 20–12 away defeat by reigning champions [[Leeds Rhinos]].  Newton went on to score his first try for the club in only his second match, a thrilling 24–22 home victory against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]].

===2009===
Newton has been a star performer during the 2009 season, including scoring two hat tricks against [[Crusaders Rugby League|Crusaders]] and Warrington Wolves.  During the season Newton has, on occasions, been used as a starting prop forward before reverting to his familiar second row role later in the match. He has scored 12 Super League tries and 3 challenge cup tries in the 2009 season, his excellent form earning him a spot in the 2009 [[Super League Dream Team]] alongside teammates [[Ben Galea]], [[Michael Dobson (rugby league)|Michael Dobson]] and [[Shaun Briscoe]].  Newton signed a four-year contract to take him through to the end of 2013 with Hull KR.<ref>{{cite news|title=Newton signs new Hull KR contract|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/super_league/hull_kr/7939323.stm|publisher=[[BBC Sport]]|date=12 March 2009|access-date=27 May 2009}}</ref>

===2010===
Following on from the successful 2009 campaign Newton continued his string of impressive performances in the opening round victory over [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]], scoring a try in the 30–12 victory.

==Penrith Panthers==
Newton signed a two-year deal with the Penrith Panthers from 2012 having been brought in by [[Phil Gould (rugby league)|Phil Gould]] to help rebuild the club, mentor younger players and use his experience to lead by example.{{citation needed|date=April 2016}}  Newton played a total of 46 first-grade games out of a possible 48 during his time at Penrith.
[[File:Clint Newton 2013.jpg|250px|thumb|right|Newton in 2013]]

==Return to Newcastle Knights==
On 6 September 2013, Newton signed a 1-year contract to return to the [[Newcastle Knights]].<ref>[http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news-display/Hilder-Tahu-Quinn-and-Newton-for-2014/83845 ] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131016022850/http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news-display/Hilder-Tahu-Quinn-and-Newton-for-2014/83845|date=16 October 2013 }}</ref>

On 21 July 2015, Newton announced his retirement from rugby league at the end of the year, after spending his two last years back at the Knights mentoring younger players in [[New South Wales Cup]].<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.newcastleknights.com.au/news/2015/07/21/clint_newton_announc.html|title=Clint Newton announces retirement – Knights|date=21 July 2015|publisher=Newcastleknights.com.au|access-date=15 October 2015}}</ref> On 27 September 2015, he captained the Knights in their [[2015 New South Wales Cup#Chart|2015 New South Wales Cup Grand Final]] win over the [[Wyong Roos]].<ref>{{cite web|author=N|url=http://www.nswrl.com.au/news/2015/09/22/vb_nsw_cup_grand_fin.html|title=VB NSW Cup Grand Final Team List|date=22 September 2015|publisher=NSWRL|access-date=15 October 2015}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|author=Brett Keeble|url=http://www.smh.com.au/rugby-league/newcastle-knights/newcastle-knights-extend-clint-newtons-career-with-nsw-cup-win-over-wyong-roos-20150927-gjw2gs.html|title=Newcastle Knights extend Clint Newton's career with NSW Cup win over Wyong Roos|date=27 September 2015|work=The Sydney Morning Herald|access-date=15 October 2015}}</ref>

==Post-rugby league career==
Newton is now the chief executive officer of the [[Rugby League Players Association]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2020/03/27/fateful-knight-how-clint-newtons-career-changed-in-an-instant/|title=Fateful Knight: How Clint Newton's career changed in an instant|publisher=National Rugby League|first=Martin|last=Lenehan|date=27 March 2020|access-date=22 April 2020}}</ref>

==Outside rugby league==
While recuperating from injury in 2008, Clint caddied for [[Nikki Garrett]] during the [[Göteborg Masters]] in Sweden.<ref>{{cite news|title=Hull KR league star Clint Newton caddying for Nikki Garrett|url=http://www.news.com.au/dailytelegraph/sport/nrl/story/0,26799,24443438-5016307,00.html|work=[[The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|The Daily Telegraph]]|location=Sydney|date=10 April 2008|access-date=10 April 2008| first=Josh| last=Massoud}}</ref>
Newton has also appeared alongside Garrett at the Jack Newton Celebrity Classic.<ref>{{cite news|title=Nikki Garrett|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/gallery/gallery-e6freye9-1111119929909?page=3|work=[[The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|The Daily Telegraph]]|location=Sydney|date=19 December 2008|access-date=19 December 2008}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Golf glamour girl Nikki Garrett all the buzz|url=http://www.dailytelegraph.com.au/sport/golf/golf-glamour-girl-nikki-garrett-all-the-buzz/comments-e6frey60-1111118358226|work=[[The Daily Telegraph (Sydney)|The Daily Telegraph]]|location=Sydney|date=19 December 2008|access-date=19 December 2008}}</ref>
  
During 2009 Clint appeared on the BBC World News show, [[Peschardt's People]], where he talked to journalist [[Michael Peschardt]] about rugby league, his life in Hull and his family.

==Career highlights==
*''Junior Club:'' Valentine-Eleebana (Newcastle)
*''First Grade Debut:'' Newcastle v Warriors at EnergyAustralia Stadium, 31 March 2001 (Round 7)
*''First Grade Record:'' 115 career NRL games to date, scoring 16 tries
*''Super League Debut:'' Leeds v Hull KR at Headingley Stadium, 2 February 2008
